coredata.dc:title: Layer-by-layer self-assembly for carbon dots/chitosan-based multilayer: Morphology, thickness and molecular interactions
coredata.dc:creator: da S. Pinto, Tarciane
Alves, Larissa A.
de Azevedo Cardozo, Gabriele
Munhoz, Victor H.O.
Verly, Rodrigo M.
Pereira, Fabiano V.
de Mesquita, João P.
coredata.dc:description: Oxidized Carbon dots, prepared from the acid dehydration of cellulose, were used in the preparation of self-assembled films with the biopolymer chitosan. The morphology, thickness and molecular interactions of the films were characterized with a variety of techniques including AFM, FTIR, UV–Vis and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) spectroscopy. We noticed an increase in the thickness of the bilayers with increasing ionic strength and pH provided by the increase of the amount of CD and chitosan deposited in each cycle. This increase was mainly due to higher flexibility of the biopolymer on these conditions and to the formation of agglommerates of carbon dots in high ionic strength. On the other hand, the greater flexibility together with the higher adsorption have generated films with smoother morphology due to the higher coating on the spherical particles. The results described here suggest that the structure of the film, i.e., morphology and thickness can be easily controlled and reproduced, by controlling the conditions of the biopolymer and CD solutions, in addition to the traditional number of dips. Finally, the prepared films have potential application in the development of imunosensors, functional coatings, surface photoactive and UV protection films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin films
Nanostrucutures
Surface properties
Atomic force microscopy (AFM)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of carbon dots and chitosan solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais

Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: CAPES , FAPEMIG ( CEX-APQ-00614-13 ), ( CEX-APQ-00851-13 ), and EDT PPM IX, RQ-MG ( CEX - RED-00010-14 ) and CNPq (grants # 478228/2013-9 ) for the financial support. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the CD prepared from acid dehydratation/oxidation.
Images of CD solutions under ambient (a) and UV (b) light.

Sensogram of the addition of alternate layers of chitosan in acetic acid aqueous solution 3% (v/v) and CD, on a gold-silicon dioxide sensor. The first layer of chitosan was added at 18 min, followed by a CD layer. RUeq = Resonance units at equilibrium.

Sensogram of the addition of chitosan layers in acetic acid aqueous solution 3% (v/v) and pH = 3.5 (red line), at pH = 5 (black line) and in acetic acid 3% (v/v) solution with 0.1 M NaCl (blue line) on CD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–Vis spectra of the films prepared in different conditions of the chitosan solutions: a) pH = 3.5; b) pH = 5.0; c) pH = 3.5 and NaCl 0.1 M. In c) chitosan and CD solution was adjusted to 0.1 M. The spectra correspond to 0, 4, 8, 12, 16 and 20 bilayers. Insets: photographs of the films grown on glass substrate.
(a) absorbance at 400 nm as a function of the number of bilayers of CH/CD; (b) amount of CD deposited in each cycle.

Effect of the ionic strength of the chitosan (symbols black and red) and CD solutions (symbols red and blue) in the amount of CD deposited. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
AFM images Tapping-mode AFM height images of a 2 μm × 2 μm area for 20 bilayer chitosan/CD thin film grown on a glass substrate.
FTIR spectra of the CD, CH and films LBL prepared in different experimental conditions.
Amount of acid functional groups determined by potentiometric titration and the values of ζ-potential obtained at different conditions in aqueous solution.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: pH = 5.4 and ionic strength of 0.1 M.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intrinsic viscosity and ζ-potential of chitosan in different solution conditions.

row.entry: Acetic acid (v/v, pH = 3.5) and NaCl (0.1 M)

head.ce:title: Layer-by-layer self-assembly for carbon dots/chitosan-based multilayer: Morphology, thickness and molecular interactions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry – FACET, Federal University of the Jequitinhonha and Mucuri Valleys, Rodovia MGT 367 - Km583, nº 5000, Alto da Jacuba, Diamantina, MG, 39100-000, Brazil.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Federal University of the Jequitinhonha and Mucuri Valleys
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Rodovia MGT 367 - Km583, nº 5000, Alto da Jacuba

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidized Carbon dots, prepared from the acid dehydration of cellulose, were used in the preparation of self-assembled films with the biopolymer chitosan. The morphology, thickness and molecular interactions of the films were characterized with a variety of techniques including AFM, FTIR, UV–Vis and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) spectroscopy. We noticed an increase in the thickness of the bilayers with increasing ionic strength and pH provided by the increase of the amount of CD and chitosan deposited in each cycle. This increase was mainly due to higher flexibility of the biopolymer on these conditions and to the formation of agglommerates of carbon dots in high ionic strength. On the other hand, the greater flexibility together with the higher adsorption have generated films with smoother morphology due to the higher coating on the spherical particles. The results described here suggest that the structure of the film, i.e., morphology and thickness can be easily controlled and reproduced, by controlling the conditions of the biopolymer and CD solutions, in addition to the traditional number of dips. Finally, the prepared films have potential application in the development of imunosensors, functional coatings, surface photoactive and UV protection films.

ce:section.ce:para: Self-assembly of soft materials have attracted a great attention in the fields related to the development of electronic, optical and photochemical devices, mainly because of their characteristic such as ease and low cost of preparation, biocompatibility and possibility of obtaining flexible materials [1]. Carbon dots (CD) is the newest class of carbon materials discovered in 2004 during the electrophoretic purification of carbon nanotubes [2]. Typically, CD exhibit sizes smaller than 10 nm, quasi-spherical morphology and structure essentially consisting of sp2 and sp3 carbon [3]. They present distinct physico-chemical, electronic and optical properties [4]. However, the mechanism for electron transfer and optical properties of CD is uncertain, but a clear dependence on the size of the nanoparticles [5] and surface properties [6,7] has been observed. In addition, the CD present biocompatibility, low toxicity, as well as good chemical and photochemical stability [3]. Thus, the potential application of these nanostructures in the development of new photocatalysts, fluorescent bioimaging (in vitro and in vivo), sensors, light-energy conversion devices and related areas has been considered.

In recent literature, various polymeric nanocomposites with carbon dots have been reported [8–14]. Chitosan (CH) is a biopolymer derived from chitin, the second most abundant organic substance on earth, after cellulose. In addition to its low toxicity, biocompatibility and antibacterial properties, this polymer behaves as a semi-flexible polyelectrolyte and shows good ability to form films [15]. Chitosan/CD nanocomposites are particularly interesting mainly because they are materials that have low toxicity and present biocompatibility. Konwar et al. investigated thermomechanical, optical and hydrophilic properties of chitosan/CD nanocomposite and verified the increased hydrophobic character and improvement of the mechanical properties [12]. On the other hand, Huang et al. prepared a sensitive and reliable dopamine (DA) biosensor based on a carbon dots and chitosan composite film modified glassy carbon electrode with the limit of detection as 11.2 nM(3S/N) [10].

The layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly technique is known by its simplicity, low cost, and versatility in manufacturing processes of highly ordered multilayer film using various types of materials [16]. The technique is based on the sequential adsorption of alternating monolayers of two components by attractive forces, e.g., electrostatic interactions [17,18]. The LbL technique is suitable for controlling the nanoscale composition with homogeneous distribution of the components, maximizing the interaction and the synergistic effects. Thus, structures with reproducible and well-defined architecture can be obtained through the generation of high precision and high performance new materials. In addition to traditional LbL films based on polymers of opposite charges, e.g., polyaniline and poly (styrene sulfonate) [19], various LbL self-assembled films have been prepared with organic and inorganic nanostructures such as clays [20], carbon nanotubes [21,22], graphene [16], cellulose nanocrystals [23–25] and a large variety of metals-based nanostructures [26].

Although nanocomposites Chitosan/CD have already been prepared, the study of the interface/compatibility between these two materials has not yet been reported. In addition, the use of carbon dots in the preparation of self-assembled multilayer films is incipient and mainly developed with inorganic structures [27–32]. Gonçalves et al. reported the development of a fiber-optic based sensor for Hg(II) ions based in a thin film of carbon dots/poly(ethyleneimine) prepared by the layer-by-layer assembly method. The sensor development was capable of selectively detecting sub-micromolar concentrations of Hg(II) with an increased sensitivity compared to carbon dot solutions [33]. Recently Chai et al. reported a facile layer-by-layer method to fabricate uniform CD/CdS heterojunction films for the formation of intimate interfacial contact improving the charge separation and transfer of the electron donor–acceptor heterojunction, contributing to enhance photoelectron-chemical and/or photocatalytic efficiency of the system [34].

Since the compatibility of the materials and morphological characteristics play an important role in the development of high performance materials, here we prepare nanocomposites of chitosan with highly oxidized carbon dots using the layer-by-layer technique with alternate deposition of the materials. We have particularly investigated the effect of pH and ionic strength on the film properties, i.e., morphology, thickness and molecular interactions between the two components to better understand the formation mechanism and the likely effect on their properties and applications. A detailed investigation of the interface between these two components is helpful and can contribute to a deeper understanding of the growth mechanism and final properties of multilayered thin films.

Cotton cellulose was purchased in the local market and chitosan (CH) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich®. The degree of deacetylation, as determined by potentiometric titration and FTIR, was greater than 69% (see Supplementary material). All other reagents were used without further purification.

The methodology of carbon dots preparation has been described elsewhere [35]. Briefly, 5 g of cotton cellulose (Apolo®) were dispersed in 20 mL of H2SO4 (Isofar®,Brazil) at 80 °C. Subsequently, the reaction was interrupted by adding 100 mL of water and the obtained carbon material was washed and re-dispersed in HNO3 solution (2 M) (Synth®, Brazil). Finally, the mixture was refluxed for 12 h, neutralized with Na2CO3 (Dinâmica®, Brazil) and dialyzed (Sigma membrane) until constant conductivity was reached (100 μS/cm). Typically, an aqueous solution with a concentration of approximately 1.0 g/L of CD was obtained.

The chitosan/CD multilayers were prepared using the traditional dip coating process on a glass or quartz slides substrates, according to a procedure described previously with some modifications [24,25]. The slides were sequentially immersed into the solutions following the procedure: 1) dipping of the substrate for 5 min in the chitosan solution 1% (m/v) prepared in acetic acid aqueous solution 3% (v/v) with pH = 3.5; (2) rinsing with deionized water (resistivity > 18,2 MΩ cm) for 2 min in three different beakers to rinse the excess of the material; (3) dipping for 5 min in a CD aqueous solution 1.2 g/L; (4) rinsing with deionized water (resistivity > 18,2 MΩ cm) with the same procedure described in (2). The entire cycle was then repeated until the desired number of bilayers (chitosan + CD) were deposited. The conformation of the polymer chains of chitosan is highly dependent on pH and ionic strength. Thus, we prepared two multilayer self-assembled films with different chitosan solutions: i) chitosan solution 1% (w/v) prepared in acetic acid aqueous solution 3% (v/v) with pH 5 adjusted with NaOH 0,1 M; ii) 1% chitosan solution (w/v) prepared in aqueous acetic acid solution 3% (v/v) in the presence of 0.1 M NaCl.

SPR measurements were performed at wavelengths of 670 and 850 nm simultaneously, at 25 °C and flow rate of 50 μL min−1 using a Multi-Parametric Surface Plasmon Resonance (MP-SPR) instrument SPRNavi 200 (BioNavis®, Finland). Experiments were carried out employing a SiO2 SPR sensor chips (SPR102-SiO2) which were washed in situ in the flow channel with sequential 5 min injections of 5% Hellmanex III®, isopropyl alcohol and milli-Q® water immediately before each experiment. After each wash, the condition of sensor chip was confirmed by init scan mode in water. The equilibrium was confirmed by measuring the running buffer (acetic acid 3% v/v in water) for approximately 10 min. The experiments consisted firstly in injecting 2 g/L of chitosan solution in acetic acid buffer. After approximately 10 min of stabilization, the CD solution (1.2 g/L) was injected. The experiment proceeded with alternate injections of the chitosan and the CD solutions. The thickness parameters were obtained by the software Winspall® (Res-Tec-Resonant Technologies® GmBH), through iterative simulations of SPR curves, based on the sensorchip's and adsorbed layers thickness and refractive indexes. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images of the CD were taken using a FEI Tecnai G2-Spirit with 200 kV acceleration voltage. The CD suspensions were deposited from an aqueous dispersion on electron microscopic carbon film grids. FTIR spectra of the materials were obtained in a Nicolet 6700 using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory (Smart iTRTM). FTIR spectra of the chitosan/CD LBL films were obtained from scrape of the films grown on glass slides. All potentiometric titration curves were performed at 25 °C, under nitrogen atmosphere, using pH electrode model N6280 (SCHOTT) coupled with a SCHOTT automatic titrator (Titroline 7000). A CO2-free solution of NaOH was used as a titrant [36–38]. Zeta potential (ζ-potential) of CD and chitosan were measured using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, UK). The intrinsic viscosity of chitosan solution was measured by glass capillary viscometer (size 50) at 25 °C. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed using a Varian Cary 50 UV–vis Spectrometer. Measurements for determination of the molar absorptivity of chitosan solutions and CD solutions were performed with 1.0 cm quartz cells, and measurements of chitosan/CD LBL films were carried out from films grown on quartz slides. The spectrum was recorded after the deposition of each two bilayers. The amount of the material deposited in each cycle was calculated using the same procedure described in our previous work [24,25]. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a MFP-3D-SA Asylum Research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots and chitosan solution
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and HRTEM images obtained for the CD indicate that the particles exhibited a quasi-spherical morphology with an average diameter of 2.7 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1 ) [38].

These CDs exhibit an absorption profile that starts at 500 nm with emission in the green region of the electromagnetic spectrum (Fig. 2 ). In addition, they exhibit optical properties dependent on the excitation wavelength [35].

The presence of large amounts of oxygenated functional groups is expected due to the dehydration reaction with H2SO4 and subsequently oxidation with HNO3, which attacks the carbon surface eroding its structure. The surface properties of CD used here were characterized by a variety of techniques including FTIR, thermogravimetric analysis, TG-MS and potentiometric titration which has been described recently [38]. Because the success in obtaining self-assembled films using the LbL technique is directly related to surface chemistry of the materials, here we show only the acidity constant and the amount of each of the acidic oxygenated functional groups determined by the potentiometric titration curves (Table 1 ). The presence of charged groups (and the charge density) are important parameters to stabilize the solution, and also to some characteristics of the film growth, such as cohesive forces and surface roughness of the prepared films [24,25]. Based on the results obtained here (Table 1), we concluded that the CD prepared here are highly oxidized (total acidic functional groups = 4.5 mmoL/g) with approximately 63% of the functional groups with pKa characteristic of carboxylic groups. At pH = 5.4, the presence of these acidic functional groups, especially carboxylic groups, is sufficient to result in the development of a potential ζ = −47 ± 17 mV. This ζ potential is lower at high ionic strength (Table 1).
Chitosan mainly shows the amino groups with acid-base properties. For chitosan used in this work, the total acidic functional groups was 4.3 mmol g−1 with a pKa estimated at 6.2.

The characteristics of self-assembled films, i.e., morphology and thickness depend on the interaction between the two components. This interaction depends on the morphology and surface chemistry of both materials, i.e. nanoparticle and polymeric material. If, on one hand, the morphology of the nanoparticles is invariable, the conformation of the polymer chain or agglomerates is highly complex and dependent on the conditions of pH and ionic strength of the medium [39–41]. Thus, we conducted a brief study of the effect of different conditions of pH and ionic strength of the solution on the surface charge and viscosity of the chitosan solution (Table 2 ). At pH = 3.5, the amino groups are fully protonated (-NH3+) generating a ζ-potential of +42 mV. As a result, there is a great intra and intermolecular repulsion of the polymer chains generating conformations that are more rigid and extended. On the other hand, at pH = 5, the low degree of protonation generates a lower ζ-potential, +7.7 mV, which allows higher intra and intermolecular interactions, thereby resulting in polymeric structures with more flexible conformations [15]. With the increase of the ionic strength of the solution at pH = 3.5 with NaCl addition (0.1 M), the ζ-potential decreases due to electrostatic screening effects. Similarly, an increase in the pH, results in an increase in the interactions between the polymer chains, intra and intermolecular, thus generating a more flexible structural conformation [39]. Typically, the polymer–solvent interactions are strong when the conformation of the polymer chain is extended [42]. Under these conditions, it is expected high intrinsic viscosity values. For a polyelectrolyte such as chitosan, the chain extension depends on the number of positive charges (-NH3+), and the number of these positive charges decrease with increasing pH and ionic strength, in such a way that the intrinsic viscosities shown in Table 2 are in agreement with expected values.

SPR experiments were performed in order to assess both, the capacity of the chitosan and CD layers to adsorb each other and the thickness of the chitosan layer, with different pH and ionic strength. For the first case, a sensogram was recorded while adding a first chitosan layer over the SiO2-functionalized SPR gold sensor and then, alternate layers of CD and chitosan. A total of 5 chitosan and 4 CD layers were added to the sensor, as shown in the sensogram depicted in Fig. 3 . This experiment evidenced that the layers have mutual affinity and their interactions were observed to be strong enough to keep the system stable over the duration of the experiment.

In the second set of experiments, SPR angles were acquired for the chitosan layers obtained from different media, as shown in Fig. 4 . During the injection of the chitosan solution (starting with 4 min) the change in SPR angle is high due to the large amount of material deposited on the surface of the sensorchip. After the injection time, the variation of the angle decreases and stabilizes from ∼12 min. The calculation of the thickness of the chitosan monolayer was performed using RU values obtained at 4 and 12 min. The layer thickness calculated for chitosan prepared in acetic acid aqueous solution 3% (v/v) and pH = 3.5 was 0.72 nm. At pH = 5, the estimated thickness increased to 1.2 nm, while at a higher ionic force (acetic acid 3% v/v solution with 0.1 M NaCl) the chitosan layer became even thicker, ranging up to 1.4 nm. At low pH and high ionic strength, the polymer chains are less charged, therefore allowing more flexible conformations and more intra- and intermolecular interactions, consequently forming larger structures that can form thicker layers.

Once the adsorption (i.e., the interaction between one of the components with other and the effect of variation of pH and ionic strength of the chitosan solution on the thickness of the deposited polymer monolayer) was verified, the films growth was also monitored by UV-VIS spectroscopy. UV–Vis absorbance spectra were obtained for the bilayers (chitosan/CD) deposited on both sides of the quartz substrate. Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra obtained with different numbers of bilayers. For all the experimental conditions there was an increased absorption with the number of bilayers deposited. In addition, it was found that with the increase of the pH (Fig. 5b) and ionic strength (Fig. 5c) led to the formation of yellowish films, suggesting an increase in the amount of material deposited. Comparing the inserts in Fig. 5a, B and 5c, it can be seen a more intense yellow coloration for the films prepared at high pH or high ionic strength, indicating that thicker films were formed on these latter conditions.

In order to verify this observation, the value of the absorption at 400 nm was plotted as a function of the number of bilayers (Fig. 6 a). This wavelength was chosen based on the absorption characteristics of the two materials. Chitosan is composed of two far UV chromophoric groups, N-acetylglucosamine and glucosamine, which contribute in an additive way to the total absorbance of the material [43,44]. On the other hand, CD absorbs a wide range of wavelengths starting from 500 nm with a strong absorption in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum. In general, electronic transitions in CD have been attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and n-π* of CO bonds (see Supplementary information) [3]. Therefore, in 400 nm, the observed absorbance is mainly attributed to CD (Figs. S3 and S4). All multilayers films presented a linear increase of absorption at 400 nm with the number of bilayers deposited, indicating the adsorption of the same amount of material in each cycle (Fig. 6). In addition, this result reflects the homogeneous formation of the multilayered films and the absence of islands/agglomerates of materials in each layer of the composite [45]. The reproducibility of the films growth was assessed and the results are shown in Fig. S5. The molar absorptivity of CD was estimated at 2.3 m2 g-1 in the wavelength of 400 nm. In this region, the molar absorptivity of chitosan is approximately zero (see Supplementary information). From this result, the absorbance values showed in Fig. 6a were converted in mass of CD deposited (Fig. 6b). From the slope of the plot, we determined the amount of 11 mg m−2, 15 mg m−2 and 22 mg m−2 of CD deposited in each adsorption cycle for samples prepared with chitosan solutions in pH = 3.5, pH = 5 and pH = 3.5 in the presence of NaCl, respectively. The increase of the deposited mass of CD with increasing pH and ionic strength may be associated with greater amount of deposited chitosan and/or to the increase of the deposited mass of CD nanoparticles at high ionic strength conditions (Fig. 5c). Several studies have shown that the conformation of the chitosan polymer chains depends on the various parameters, i.e., degree of acetylation, chain length, ionic strength, solvent, temperature, pH and dielectric constant of the solvent [46–48]. Similar results were observed in other studies in the preparation of self-assembled films of chitosan and heparin [49,50]. According to these authors, the increase in amount of deposited material and thickness are related to conformation of the chitosan polymer chain in different conditions of pH and ionic strength. The heparin is a strong polyanion, which adsorbs in an extended conformation, leading to thinner layers.

In contrast, being a weak polycation, it is expected that chitosan can be more affected by changes in the pH and ionic strength. Boddohi et al. prepared layer-by-layer films between heparin (a strong polyanion) and chitosan (a weak polycation) and verified that at 0.2 M buffer, the thickness of the films could be increased from less than 2 nm per bilayer to greater than 4 nm per bilayer, by varying the solution pH [50]. On the other hand, Almodóvar et al. verified that hydrophilicity and swelling are reduced when one polyelectrolyte is strong (e.g. N,N,N-trimethyl chitosan) and the other is a weak one (e.g. hyaluronan), whereas ion pairing is increased [51].

In this study, both, CD and chitosan have only weakly acidic groups. Thus, their surface charges are highly affected by the conditions of pH and ionic strength of the solution. However, the carbon nanoparticle structure (“conformation”) is invariable. To evaluate the effect of the CD surface charge and also the ionic strength of the chitosan solution on the amount of deposited material, LbL films were prepared under different conditions of ionic strength of the CD solution and chitosan solution. The spectra obtained for the films prepared with CD solution at pH = 6 and chitosan solutions at different ionic strength are shown in Fig. S6. The deposited masses obtained for each of these films are shown in Fig. 7 (symbols black and red). In this graph is also shown the amount of CD deposited when the ionic strength of both the solutions were adjusted to 0.1 M (symbol blue). The amounts of deposited material were 11 mg m−2, 16 mg m−2 and 22 mg m−2 for the films prepared with different ionic strength of the aqueous solutions. It is clear that increasing the ionic strength of the chitosan solution increases the amount of deposited material (symbols black and red in Fig. 7). This quantity is even greater when the ionic strength of both solutions are increased, showing that the ionic strength of the CD solution increases the amount of carbon dots deposited on each layer.

Thus, the increase of the deposited material is related to an increase in polymer chain flexibility at high pH and ionic strength. Because of the greater flexibility of the polymer on these conditions, the polymeric chains adopt a more appropriate conformation for adsorption on the quasi-spherical surface morphology of the CD, generating more compact and thick films, although on these conditions there is a decrease in electrostatic interaction due to the reduced surface charge of the polymer (Table 2). The increased flexibility of the polymeric chains, which results in a greater adhesion on the nanostructures, plays an important role in the mechanical properties of nanocomposites obtained by the layer-by-layer deposition technique [52]. On the other hand, the presence of ions in solution strongly influences the stability of suspensions of carbon nanostructures [53]. The increasing ionic strength of CD suspension can screen the CD surface charge, reducing ζ-potential of the nanoparticles (Table 1) allowing a greater interaction between them and consequently a higher aggregation. However, in the conditions used, the electrostatic repulsion is still higher than the van der Waals attractive force, thus inhibiting the precipitation. As a result, the adsorption of CD agglomerates occurs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used to examine the surface structure and thickness of self-assembled films obtained from chitosan/CD. Generally, the film supported onto glass slides resulting in a mountain-and-valley structure (Fig. 8 ). However, the mountains-and-valleys topographies observed in the samples are quite different. In the film prepared in low pH and low ionic strength (Fig. 8a), it is possible to observe very small particles that form a very rugged topography, but with low amplitudes. The average roughness (RMS) of 11 nm was obtained for a film thickness of approximately 60 nm (for 20 bilayers), (Fig. S7). Since the amount of material deposited in these conditions is very small, this result suggests that the isolated CD or low degree of agglomeration of these nanoparticles is adsorbed. Moreover, the coating of carbon nanoparticles is very thin, allowing the observation of very small particles. With the increase of pH to 5 (Fig. 8b), the morphology of the film is presented in the form of larger spherical agglomerates than those observed in Fig. 8a, suggesting the adsorption of CD agglomerates and a more effective coating on the CD (formed by chitosan polymer), due to the greater flexibility of the chain polymer. On the other hand, the flexible polymer chains on these conditions may strongly interact with larger CD agglomerates, contributing to the formation of thicker films. The average thickness of the film was ∼150 nm (for 20 bilayers), with a Root Mean Square (RMS) roughness = 28 nm (Fig. S8). Moreover, the film prepared with higher ionic strength of the chitosan and CD suspension in the presence of 0.1 M NaCl (Fig. 8c) shows a smoother topography, with almost complete disappearance of spherical particles, suggesting that under these conditions the polymer chains, more flexible, adopt appropriate conformations to maximize interaction with CD agglomerates and consequently much more material is deposited during the film growth. The thickness of the film prepared under these conditions was ∼200 nm (for 20 bilayers) with an average roughness of 37 nm (Fig. S9).

SPR experiment showed that the layers CH/CD have mutual affinity and their interactions are strong enough to keep the system stable with the deposition of multiple layers. However, to evaluate a particular interaction between the components of the film, we used FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 9 shows the spectra of the two components used in the preparation, as well as the LbL films obtained in different experimental conditions. The spectra profile obtained for the chitosan biopolymer and the carbon dots are typical of these compounds. The spectra of the films show the characteristic bands of both [35,54,55]. In particular it is possible to observe the absorption of the saccharide structure of the biopolymer around 1000 cm−1 and different functional groups of the CD around 1550 cm−1. Moreover, it was not possible to verify significant changes in the spectra, for example, -NH3+ chitosan groups with COOH of the CD, which show some interaction between them since both chitosan and CD present absorptions in the same regions of the FTIR spectrum.

The absorptivity values of the CDs obtained in this work (see Supplementary material) are compared with organic molecules traditionally used as sun protectors. Therefore we believe that the films prepared here can be potentially used as UV protectors. In this context our research group is working on optimizing the UV protection properties of the CDs to achieve higher absorption and consequently, higher protection factors.

The deposition layer-by-layer technique was successfully performed in the preparation of new self-assembled multilayer films based on chitosan and CD. For all the experimental conditions used for the growth of the multilayer films, we have observed a linear film growth with the number of dips performed. However, different conditions of the biopolymer chitosan and CD suspension, i.e. pH and ionic strength, produced films with different thickness and morphology. We found that at high pH and ionic strengths values, there was a greater amount of deposited material on the previous layer leading to the formation of thicker films. The amount of CD deposited by bilayer (CH/CD) with the chitosan solution at pH = 3.5, pH = 5 and pH = 3.5 in the presence of 0.1 M NaCl were 11 mg m−2, 16 mg m−2 and 24 mg m−2, respectively. The thicknesses of the films corresponding to these amounts of deposited material were 50, 140 and 200 nm, for 20 bilayers. We believe that this result is related to an increased flexibility of the polymeric chains of the biopolymer, covering more efficiently the structure of the quasi-spherical nanoparticles. In addition, increased ionic strength leads to deposition of a greater amount of CD because of the formation of agglomerates under these conditions. The results suggest that the structure of the film, i.e., morphology and thickness can be easily controlled and reproduced by controlling the conditions of the biopolymer solution, in addition to the traditional number of dips. The results shown here can contribute to future investigations on new architectures based on LbL films built with carbon dots for the development of new design of nanodevices for applications in imunosensors, photoactive surfaces, functional coatings, UV protection films and devices related to energy production and conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: CAPES, FAPEMIG (CEX-APQ-00614-13), (CEX-APQ-00851-13), and EDT PPM IX, RQ-MG (CEX - RED-00010-14) and CNPq (grants # 478228/2013-9) for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2016.10.032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biobased nanocomposites from layer-by-layer assembly of cellulose nanowhiskers with chitosan
